title: Religious Sites & Temple Etiquette in China

tips.tip: Cover shoulders and knees at temples
tips.detail: Bring a scarf or light jacket to cover exposed skin at Buddhist and Taoist temples. Avoid short skirts, shorts, and sleeveless tops.

tips.tip: Remove hats when entering main halls
tips.detail: Take off hats, caps, and sunglasses when entering the main worship hall as a sign of respect to the deities.

tips.tip: Remove shoes at the threshold if required
tips.detail: Some temples provide shelves for shoes. Look for signs or follow locals. Step over the door frame rather than on it.

tips.tip: Wear clean socks and easy-to-remove shoes
tips.detail: Since you may need to remove shoes frequently, wear presentable socks and slip-on footwear for convenience.
Many temples prohibit photography inside worship halls. Look for signs and always ask permission before photographing monks or worshippers.

tips.tip: Never use flash photography near Buddha statues
tips.detail: Flash can damage ancient gilding and paintings. Even where photos are allowed, always disable your flash.
Fruit, flowers, and packaged vegetarian snacks are suitable offerings. Never bring meat to a Buddhist temple.

tips.tip: Hold incense properly with both hands
tips.detail: Light incense with the temple's candle, never from another person's stick. Hold it upright and bow three times before placing it in the burner.

tips.tip: Do not blow out incense or candles with your mouth
tips.detail: Wafer or wave gently to extinguish flames. Blowing is considered disrespectful as the mouth carries impure breath.

tips.tip: Bow or kneel respectfully when greeting Buddha statues
tips.detail: A slight bow with palms together is appropriate. If you wish to pray, kneel on the cushion and bow three times with hands in prayer position.
Use 'Fashi' (法师) for Buddhist monks or 'Shifu' (师傅). Avoid touching monks, especially if you are of a different gender. Do not initiate handshakes.

tips.tip: Never touch or point at sacred statues or objects
tips.detail: Do not touch Buddha statues, religious texts, or ritual objects unless explicitly invited to do so. Pointing with a single finger is rude; use an open hand instead.

tips.tip: Pay entrance fees and donations without complaint
tips.detail: Many temples charge an entrance fee that supports preservation. Small donation boxes are also common. Carry small bills and coins.
Speak in hushed tones. Do not laugh loudly, run, or make phone calls inside temple grounds. Silence your mobile phone.
Do not walk in front of someone who is praying. Wait for them to finish before passing. Do not sit in areas marked for worshippers only.
